summary: A data scientist father discovered that his children's elementary school had been incorrectly rated by GreatSchools.org for years due to a publishing error. Despite the low rating, his family's personal experience was positive, yet he watched neighbors avoid or leave the school based solely on the single-digit score. After persistent emails, GreatSchools acknowledged their error and corrected the rating from 5 to 8. The post illustrates how algorithmic ratings can create self-fulfilling prophecies that damage communities, even when the underlying data is simply wrong.
key_points: Parents routinely trust algorithmic school ratings over their own positive personal experiences
The low rating was caused by a publishing system error, not actual school performance
Families avoided or fled the school based on a single erroneous digit, causing real community harm
The author had to persistently push back against corporate cut-and-paste responses to get the error acknowledged
The corrected rating now raises questions about whether 'bandwagon parents' will suddenly view the same school differently
money_quotes: It was sad to see people trust a single digit score more than a personal experience.
When they would move, we'd ask what in the school do you dislike and they would acknowledge their personal experience was positive but they wanted to move to a 'better' school.
The perception that Quatama is a low performing school was completely erroneous and based off a math system gone wrong.
My thought that the same way there are bandwagon fans, there are bandwagon parents.
This will open up a large question about the morality of publishing overly simplified data.
key_insight: A simple publishing error in a school rating algorithm drove families away from a quality school for years, demonstrating how algorithmic systems can inflict real damage on communities even when they're just plain wrong.
